title: Gate Parameter Lee-Yang Zeros and Dynamical Phases in Quantum Circuits
authors: Chang Liu, Yu Wu, Yunfeng Jiang, Yang Zhang
abstract: We propose gate-parameter Lee-Yang zeros of Loschmidt amplitudes as probes of dynamical phases in finite quantum circuits. We illustrate this approach using a brickwork model, where the time evolution is generated by repeated application of a Floquet operator. The Loschmidt amplitude can be expressed as a rational function of the gate parameters. At fixed system size and large circuit depth, its zeros in one complexified gate parameter, with the other parameter held fixed, condense onto limiting curves. We show that these curves comprise a universal component governed by equimodular Floquet eigenvalues, as described by the Beraha-Kahane-Weiss theorem, together with state-dependent contributions controlled by the overlap of eigenstate of the Floquet operator with the initial state. As one of the parameters is varied, the set of zeros reorganizes abruptly, providing a finite-qubit diagnostic of a dynamical phase transition. This mechanism does not rely on integrability: while integrability enables an exact calculation of the Loschmidt amplitude, the condensation of zeros follows from spectral competition and local unitarity alone.
summary: The authors introduce gate-parameter Lee-Yang zeros of the Loschmidt amplitude as a novel diagnostic tool for detecting dynamical phase transitions in finite quantum circuits. By analyzing how these zeros condense in the complex parameter plane, they show that an abrupt reorganization signals a phase transition, a mechanism that works even when the system is not integrable.
main_problem: The paper proposes using gate-parameter Lee-Yang (GPLY) zeros of Loschmidt amplitudes to diagnose dynamical phase transitions in finite quantum circuits.
main_result: The condensation of these zeros onto limiting curves provides a finite-qubit diagnostic for dynamical phase transitions, independent of the system's integrability.
method: The analysis involves calculating the Loschmidt amplitude, which is treated as a rational function of complexified gate parameters, and studying the accumulation points (zeros) of this function.
model_or_system: The study uses quantum circuits, specifically illustrated with a brickwork model whose time evolution is generated by a Floquet operator. The underlying physics is analyzed in terms of spectral competition and local unitarity.
key_observables: Gate-parameter Lee-Yang (GPLY) zeros of the Loschmidt amplitude, and the zero-density diagnostic $R(\Delta; \epsilon, n)$.
important_parameters: Gate parameters ($\alpha, \phi$), circuit depth ($n$), system size ($L$), and the spectral parameter $\Delta$, which controls the transition between massive and massless regimes.
main_assumptions: The Loschmidt amplitude must be expressible as a rational function of the gate parameters, and the analysis relies on the existence of universal components governed by Floquet eigenvalues.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the GPLY zeros in the massive and massless regimes, showing distinct geometric structures (compact curves vs. extended radial structures) around the critical point $\Delta=1$.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the GPLY zero concept using the Loschmidt amplitude for a brickwork model. It then analyzes the zero condensation in the large depth limit, distinguishing between universal (Floquet-eigenvalue based) and state-dependent zero components, culminating in the diagnosis of a phase transition at $\Delta=1$.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, non-integrability-dependent diagnostic tool for characterizing quantum phase transitions in finite quantum systems, which is highly relevant for understanding decoherence and thermalization in quantum computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 5. Zero-density diagnostic near the universal curves. The plotted quantity R(∆; ϵ, n) is the fraction of zeros within distance ϵ = 10−2 of the expected universal set: the coordi- nate axes for ∆< 1 and the unit circle for ∆> 1.
